Hey everybody, welcome back to our jam-packed week of Habertology. This is webinar number two of four, scheduled for the biggest holiday weekend in the U.S. just about. So we are really rocking and rolling and we are fully international today because Kevin and Albert Bond, Albert on the West Coast, Mark McCray in Australia and live from Pat B. We have Big Jason Henderson who has come in to talk about all things email marketing with us today and I am sure we are going to have a fast and furious conversation. I know that you've got a long-standing relationship with Bond and Kevin and many of the Albert protegees. So guys, do you want to take it away or do you want me to start entirely up to you? I'd like to introduce Jason. Absolutely. I want everybody to know, I actually first met Big Jason and it was at a John Carlton. It was a little event for people who had won a mail, you know, done really well for mailing for him. And I was hanging out with Big Jason and then I met him and you know, we were hanging out there and had some good times but we also were at another event and one of the things that was amazing to me about him was, and I mentioned this on an earlier Albertology program where a lot of people will only take big ideas or good content from somebody who they recognize as a well-respected source. And I said, you know, one of the things that we do is since we've been in the game so long we kind of can just take ideas for on their own merit and we can test things and we play with things and we don't just assume that whatever the common thinking is always correct. And Big Jason and I were both sitting there listening to some, I forget who it was, but some guru talking about email marketing and how you should never insert pictures into your email, ever. And the only other, the only two people shaking their head was Big Jason and I. We're like, no, that doesn't work because I took some advice and I inserted an image, you know, I was doing an email and I said, if I was a prospect, I'd really want an image of this kind of thing to really convey the power. So I popped that in and it increased percentages 400, click through 400 percent. And then I got with Big Jason and he started teaching me some tricks that I'll mention that we'll bring up in the middle of this for content, you know, just talking about, you know, specific things that you do. But he started in boosting my response right away and without, you know, knowing who he was and what he was doing, I just latched on like, okay, he's a go to guy for a lot of testing because Big Jason tests and the reason his info is so valuable is it's very up to date with email marketing. What comes in and out of fashion is really faster than almost any other kind of part of direct response marketing because like I said, at one moment, there, you know, it is a bad idea to send images because they thought, you know, there might have something, you've attached something nefarious to the email, then quickly it became a very acceptable and became a thing to do. I really, I don't even answer the question anymore. And the reason that question is what's the best email service provider at the moment? And the reason I say at the moment is this changes. Email service providers, you know, they're born, these are the people who handle your your big large mailing list and you, you know, allow you to mail out to thousands of people at a time. And they start giving you great customer service and their interfaces get adapted over time, but their rep, their servers can get reputations as good and bad. And what's working great last year might not necessarily be what's working terrific right now. So very specifically, what's the best service that you would recommend right now? That's a good question and you're right. It does change and I'm always searching. But we have folks that come in and just pick this up after the fact. So people send in some things. And what I'm curious about, social media has become the big buzz. Everyone's like, oh, you've got a Facebook, you've got Twitter, and HootSweets, all the mom. What do you think that that means with regard to email marketing? Do you think that email marketing will survive the social media onslaught? No doubt. That's a pretty funny question. I think it's a pretty silly argument one way or the other. I think both are awesome and they both have their place. And you can't just rely on one or the other. My game plan is always when I come to a project is, you know, how many channels do we have available to reach a target audience? Because you can't assume one is always going to reach them all the time. I'd like to add to that. Very true. And it integrated the campaign. Oh, there's a delay. So if you see why I'm interrupting somebody or this goes on and it always feels like I'm trying, I'm never trying to interrupt. But one of the things I also want to add is, if you have a company, Macintosh or Apple or any kind of service or anything, even after you've done your promotion, you take orders, you do everything automatically, your most personal communication is usually your email. And that's not going to change for a long time as well. And so we're going to talk about some personalization techniques and how that work because Big Jason's developed some great ones that I've seen other people mimicking. And I know that he's the first one that I saw doing it. And the one of the reasons I don't think email is ever going to die in that way is and maybe they'll be replaced by something that's more like mass text messaging or something like that. But right now, would you shut off your email account? You know, say, okay, I've got it. I've got social media. People can reach me through there. I've got a phone. I've got a cell phone. Would you shut off email? Never. And the reason is it's a very safe, anonymous, easy way to keep in touch with somebody and not feel the pressure of, oh, they saw my message. They saw that I saw the message. They got to get back to them right away. And it's kind of like, will the telephone go away? It may take a different form, but I don't think that voice communication is going to go away anytime soon. And Bob was talking about me and testing. Well, a lot of people are scared of testing. They feel like they don't have a big enough list to make it matter. But so the second best thing is you can at least be tracking everything. And so that's what I do a lot in social media. And since probably 2009, especially on big product launches or people with big lists, the revenue you're going to make through social media versus email is basically nil. I still make quite a few sales. Like I just did a recent joint promo with a million dollar Mike Morgan for simple writing system coaching. And most of our sales was through social media. But on average, it's not that much. But I would still not say disregard social media because I think anecdotally, I think it helps with the relationship. And again, the more channels you have, the better. I also think that if you're on social media, this is another key to really good marketing that you'll see a lot of the top marketers do. Work them into both. Do something on social media that allows you to capture a snail mail address for a physical mail, capture their email addresses and they go sign up for something. Because there's a level of connection and concentration and attention you get with these different mediums. If you've got somebody and they're reading a letter, a physical letter, you have their attention. The doorbell has to ring. The kids have to walk in or something's got to happen for them to all of a sudden have to throw that down. Social media, you're streaming amongst a whole bunch of stuff that you're checking out and people, it likes are popping up and things are going on. So once somebody's actually opened that email, you've got their attention span for a little while. There's some tricks to getting that email open, which we should go into. I think you have their attention. People talk about length of copy a lot. And a lot of that also depends on what's the norm for what you're looking at. And I think that the length of copy and emails can quite easily be much longer than social media posts and things like that. And that one-on-one connection. Your plan should figure out ways to intermix it. You see guys giving away their books all the time. And a lot of times they're doing that because they're getting your snail mail address and they're going to market to you something later. And it's worth them to send them a book that costs them $2.50 to produce because they know that they're going to be hitting you up for coaching programs and things that cost a lot more. Sure. This makes a really good point with the follow-up. I was going to ask when you talk about how ways to get it open, one of the things that social media and the advent of smartphones in particular is done is make it so that we have to, at least to my mind, we have to structure our emails a little bit differently. Have you found that to be the case? And if so, what are you finding if anything needs to be changed to go from the laptop slash PC world to the smartphone world? Oh, okay. So you're talking about how the emails look on desktop versus mobile? Yeah. How, you know, what you're dealing with with everything from coding images to just layout. Sure. I think a huge mistake and a huge waste of time is people are still under the deception that you want your emails to be like one little narrow column. And the reason how people tricks other marketers into doing that was like, oh, you know, look at a newspaper. But if you look at any newspaper from, you know, over 10 years ago to now, you never see a newspaper with one little column. There's always multiple columns that have pictures too. And just one, it wastes a lot of time trying to format it like that. And two, unless you're going to make it like 45 characters, it's going to look like crap on the mobile phone. So me personally, I like to just design a simple response of HTML email that will resize on both desktop and mobile. Because yeah, when you talk about mobile, more and more people are reading the emails on the mobile phone. And I've seen at least three studies where the number one reason why people delete emails on their phone is because they can't read it. And there's a lot of free resources. Campaign Monitor has a lot of free mobile responsive email resources. Zerb.com forward slash blog. They actually have some free email templates. I've designed some templates myself. And basically what I have done is I've gone through Zerb.com forward slash blog. And basically hacked it together to make it look like what I wanted experimented over the years to make it better and better. So yeah, it's definitely something people should be aware of is making sure emails are readable on mobile. Do you have a particular font? Go ahead. He wants to know if you have a favorite. Font. Favorite font. I would say. And font size. My everybody wants to typically default font. It's aerial. So what I like to do sometimes like currently right now I'm writing for self defense and I'm writing affiliate series for a huge partner. And they do aerial. So I'm turning in all my emails as Georgia. I like the look of it. It basically just stands out a little more than aerial. When you gave us a template it was like a Google web font. Was it not? Yeah. And which I noticed it had like smoothing and I was thought it was able to stay really clean looking no matter what device it showed up on. Yep. And Bon you were saying size. Depending on the market. I would say the fault size for me is I like uh anywhere between 16 and 18 and I think for older audiences at least 18. That's 18 pixels. I think that's probably quite a surprise to some folks in the audience because I know that everybody has their own special font size. They think gets the you know gets the traffic better. So it's always an individualized thing. But when you've got somebody like Jason who is phenomenally successful with this. Pay attention. So does anybody else want to chime in with anything with regard to email structure, fonts, smartphones versus laptops, etc. I want to make it clear that one of the things big Jason is mentioning or telling you to get is what they call dynamic email. And so what will happen is if the email is comes in on a smartphone, it'll be like this. And then if it comes in on an iPad, it'll be like that. And then if it's on a desktop, it'll come in with a limit. And the reason you want that limit is what and this is by the way stuff I've learned a lot from big Jason is it's eye fatigue. You don't want to you know you don't with the big letter box monitors, you don't want to drive. You know break somebody's neck making them do this. Because remember a lot of this is about the decision to read. And so if you people open up an email and they start reading down in the email and they get to the bottom where they have to take their mouse or their finger and scroll. And that scroll bar appears and it shows that they've only touched one 20th or one 100th of the entire email. They're now trying to decide do I have enough time to read this entire email. So there's you know so you know if you're writing a real long email and you have any kind of sort of rule of thumb for when you will make them click to read more and take them out of take them from the email to a web page. No and just one thing to add to that is like yeah I totally agree. I mean you have one side of the fence where they're making their emails you know super narrow based on you know a silly comparison to a newspaper where you got on the desktop you know probably like 600 pixels of white space you know I find that hard to read. I know a lot of people do but then yeah you're right there's the exact opposite where they have no width control and it goes all the way across the screen I find that hard to read as well. So on that for the desktop I usually go around between 600 and 620 pixels across so that's usually the max. Great and then as far as length you know you know the old saying you know about the skirts. That's typically what it is I don't have any hard fast rule. Some emails based on what I'm doing it's going to be short, pithy. My probably my most responsive email of 2013 when I was working for the number one golf site in the world revolution golf it was I think 38 words but every word counted. It was basically I ripped off your dad did an if then statement and restated the value proposition why they should click in the call to action and that did really well and then I've done a product launch for my Koenig's high seven figures where I had an affiliate complaining about how long my swipes were. I was writing all the affiliate swipes all the launch emails and I knew the guy so I was like all right so I've got a million affiliates no offense but you're not a super affiliate so I'm trying to give everybody enough to go on and you can customize it and I said second I said are you sure that your list loves short emails or you just suck at writing long emails and I was like why don't you just test it so he tested it and my longer swipe beat his short email I think it was five times the amount of sales. I can watch it. It depends especially when you're doing affiliate promos it depends on sometimes the who's writing the sales letter like for a million dollar million dollar Mike Morgan I want to get them on the sales letter you know probably sooner than later just because his copy is so brilliant but if you're just doing a favor for a friend you're looking up the sales letter it's like and you really know your list there's no reason why you can't try and sell the product in your email where they're just like yeah I want it. I'm going to click. My question is spam rules new spam laws what's the latest on either of those? Spam rules spam laws it's generally the same it's a really bad it's really bad advice to misspell words you know spam assassin they knew about that long time ago they even specified do not misspell words and just makes you look stupid. It's still you know your reputation is more important than the content it doesn't mean you can just say the word free 50 times or money 50 times or invest in it 50 times so I generally say you know responses what counts so even if it was true saying the word free would hurt your deliverability on that one email just a little bit it increases your open rate you know by a certain point your response and clicks then you know I'll take that but again it's really not about the words it's your reputation how many spam complaints you're getting so I wouldn't I don't worry worry too much about content. telling it max width is 100 so basically I'm telling it even if this image is wider than a mobile phone to not go past 100 of the screen so that's going to make it resize for you know the iphone and all that good stuff and then text align center so the alt tag if images are turned off it'll it basically aligns the alt tag so if the image is right here the text is going to be right there so that basically to me makes it stand out more and then the last one display block that's important for especially gmail that makes so if that image is turned off if you don't have that what could happen is is even if you have a border too it can be really tiny even with the border but if you have display block on then you can't see the image if images are turned off but you'll see the full width and height of the image and then you have the border of one so it really stands out and they're like huh what's this and a better chance of them getting to turn images on and just to make that very clear address and when you go check your spam email address to get that report or the confirmation link you find 500 to 1000 emails have piled up there and you go right to the top and you start to see if that this person has helped you or not and they've either given you what you want you're off to try it or they have and they're off to go look for the solution at another site on the internet either way they ignore 500 to 1000 emails right before they get to you know on their way out of their spam email address but later on when you go to your primary email address you don't just delete the spam folder that's got 20 messages in it you scan it to make sure grandma or somebody else didn't slip through the cracks and the moral of that that I like to tell people is it's better for your email to end up in the spam box of a primary email address than to end up in the primary box of a spam email address so always at the very beginning before you start anything shoot for designing things that will get you a primary email address now one of the keys to that there's a lot of keys to that in my opinion one is sharing and getting them to really understand what they're going to get and want to so if you're signing up for alerts on where your favorite band is going to be playing or when they might want to send out tickets this is another key make them time sensitive you want that so you since you don't want to miss that you feel that it's time sensitive you won't give them a spam email address you'll go ahead and give them your primary or at least you're much more likely to but another thing is mobile you know if you can design campaigns where content is just you know they want the content for their mobile device or mobile phone most people to right now cannot do not operate their spam email address on their mobile phone they usually are more comfortable with only one address being associated with their phone or the mobile device and it's usually a primary one but there want me to add something on what you just talked about the primary email address so yeah what I've noticed for myself and clients is the opt-in page is where you're trying to basically be blind as possible and get as many leads as possible in that's where I typically see a lot of the crazy looking email addresses that you're talking about and where you actually put some effort into it where you're trying to qualify your leads and you're really being detailed on what what they're going to get that's where I see the best I can visually see that I'm getting you know their first name last name type of emails a lot of a lot higher quality that's just been my you know what I've observed I know absolutely and I think if you know and you can always show them a preview you know if let's suppose you're you're offering a service that you know offers custom bids or custom offers on used cars in your area whatever you can put that out there and just kind of blur out the important parts you don't want people to see and they'll see it as a preview and understand what they're going to be getting you know people sign up and give us the primary email address of the Gary Halberlatter because they want it and it's usually also you know there's a there's a balance but softer squeeze will get you you know the the less you're pressuring the squeeze and that means you know hard squeeze is look I've got your solution you can't get anything from me without an email address period the soft squeeze is hey do you like this want some more here's an email you know just sign up and if you do that your addresses are better and one other thing that I found that I do is it is when people put in their email addresses they will go in and not capitalize their first name or they will go in and you know there'll be a typo in there because they're just quickly filling it out and one of the things that I will do is actually go through the when people sign up you know I will go through and kind of clean those up a little bit you know I because which we're going to talk about well we'll use those names and personalization later and it's you know it seems incongruent that everything's right but it says dear bond and the bond is a lowercase b so I'll go in there and there and I think there are also programs that'll make sure the first letter is uppercase and kind of filter that out but I'll fix typos you know and you know and from the email sometimes they'll leave no first name but I can see from the email address what their first name you know because it's Matt 7492 at so-and-so.com so you know so I'll change it I'll add their first name and say Matt so when they get the email and I've written hi Matt or you know they get that and so that was just a little thing I wanted to add let's so if I had anything else on getting those emails open besides the primary email address yeah so first of all you shouldn't let people's fears failures sexual inadequacies be projected on to you that's what I see when it comes to open rates especially more and more lately is is that you hear the same parroted information oh you can't deposit opens or clicks in your bank account well I call bullshit you can't deposit open rates or click the rates in your bank account because you suck you don't you don't really have the pulse of your market of your list you don't really know what they want you just blindly just throwing crap out there and yeah you can't see a really good difference on your open rate I see a time and time again when I see my clients and the goals yes is not to get you know 90% of your list to open every single email that'd be great but that's not the goal and yeah you can try and trick people to get a high open rate and that's not going to equal sales but if you really hit that nail on the head with your open rate you can increase opens and sales the problem is like I said trying to trick people so how are you going to do that how are you going to give them more people to open that qualified so it's basically like a mindset you know you got to be looking at their inbox you got to be in your competitors inboxes what are they seeing you got to put yourself in their position as you're going through you know 50 emails and deciding which one you're going to open as Kevin says you got to imagine they've got a p they got the baby crying or you know they got to eat their wife is calling them to watch a movie UFC fight's about to start what's going to stop them in the tracks to get to read your email and typically what I find it's a combination of curiosity plus relevance so a lot of people try to use blind curiosity and basically what that tells to me from my experience is is that they're trying to get every single person open up an email which I think is a big mistake you know my tests my collapse tests find that you're going to get a lot more high quality subs opens and possibly if you really hit it on the head even more opens and more sales if you combine curiosity plus relevance so you know the three things guaranteed to increase your open rate overnight versus you know hey or OMG or bad news like in my courses on email marketing probably 90% of the information is based on what works for my clients and umpteen different niches because I know for myself and a lot of the people like Frank Kern John Reese we've got built in authority okay so for me people on my free weekly show lists because they're trying to spy on me and see everything I do and copy all my emails so I know just because my email online does really well doesn't mean it's gonna do well for say a newbie coming off the warrior form because I got that built in authority and there's a lot of people telling them to be studying me and on my lists so you can't just be blindly copying people like I interviewed or um asked questions at a conference where the top two guys for a bomb is team everybody should know about their huge email campaign during the election and I said you know did you use hey and all those personal type emails trying to make them think it was a personal email from the president did you try that with anybody else and I'm like yeah I said bomb you know it was specific to Obama because from Obama yeah those worked but wasn't it was a waste of time I didn't try it so that'd be my recommendation is the real curiosity plus a relevant benefit and I've seen you use personalization in subject lines can you talk about any of the effect that's had or is that has up and you know I can't tell what's working what's not from my end but I see I've seen stuff you've done yeah um even a Weber's tested personalization and again you know these people parroting information claim that personalize personalization all the time in a subject line it's it's more for getting their attention you know they got this inbox and all you would test personalization for is getting their attention from open rate which tracks you know someone opens an email like 20 times or 25 times that counts so I'm talking tracking unique opens unique clicks and revenue per email and revenue revenue per click should be based on how often you want to hear from them you know so I don't want to hear from my plumber every day so if you've got a list that's all about plumbing you know don't don't send me an email every single day my toilet's not backed up every single day um but the but I do so I think it depends on what you're providing what your business is but my rule of thumb in general is mail till you're sure that they're not going to forget who you are because one of the biggest problems with email lists is if they let them go stale you know they sit dormant for you know you haven't mailed your list in six months and they forget who you are then you email them everybody hits spam you know and of course you can try and remind them who you are why they signed up but they hit spam and then them a deal every day right but talk to us about timing and and the number of emails in general like and what's been your experience like with just radically too much or radically too little yeah the fun thing is that when you hear someone say I switched to daily and I'm making more money and yada yada yada you never hear um how little they were emailing so you never know if it's because they switched to daily or it's just way more frequent if they've been only emailing their list twice a month and now they're mailing daily duh of course you're getting to me in my experience that's not you know that's not magic it's not the daily it's the more frequent I think from my experience that it's safe to say that probably 90% of email marketers if you're not doing daily already most email marketers are not mailing enough the frequency is not there you know they're not top of mind they're forgetting about you the forgetting why um you know maybe they're really interested in what you have to say and what's your why they signed up but you haven't emailed them in so long that they really they've moved on and you don't want that to happen so um me personally I set it up where I tell them in my welcome email that hey you know I could be emailing you daily or every other day just expect that to happen um and that's the key because you got to set the expectations what about that never have but just based on my research um I discovered that you know people would kill to be able to do that but it's just not so that attainable but it was mentioned in the sales letter and that was a subject line that got uh 180 no 166 000 people open well I play golf and I love that sound line what what about these emoticons you see in the subject lines um emoticons yeah happy faces sad five and par two in the golfer that's a dream world but it's also got a specificness to it by putting those numbers but it's also so it's got specificness benefit and a massive amount of curiosity which is usually the winning combination um you know I've mentioned it before one of my best performing subject lines was the holy trinity of copywriting books which has everything he's talking about is specific specificity being trinity you expect three um massive curiosity and being very relevant to the list because they're all copywriters but one of the things that I think is also important and then this is going to lead me to my next question is a lot of people don't understand people some people are motivated by more curiosity and some people are motivated more by benefit and you know a lot of times when you're mailing to sell something you don't want to mail just wants to your list you know this I'm not talking about you know mailing a different offer the next day I'm talking about taking the same offer and you know mailing it out with a curiosity driven subject line and my personal formula I like curiosity first then the second one I like to be more specific and then I like to hit the third or the fourth one with some sort of sense of urgency that lets them know that the emails will be ending because if they've been ignoring the emails they got to open this one and if they've been they don't like the emails they know that this email string is coming to an end and so you know I mean in general let's suppose I create an offer to my list and it's the same it's an going to how about reopens and scrubbing can you talk or not excuse me not open some scrubbing no open scrubbing yeah so when I go use you know I've been to marketing sherpa email summit you know owned by mech lab since 2006 over 10 years I spoke there I've never had seen one company that says they get rid of emails it's always tagging and I think that's a little bit extreme especially for you know a small business owner entrepreneur who maybe doesn't have the huge budget that can just keep all that dead weight for a long period of time but I think people just arbitrarily just dump you know good leads for no for no reason just based on assumptions so um I really like reactivation campaigns first before deciding whether you're going to delete them or not and sometimes if you have a big enough list more than one reactivation campaign could be worthwhile and the key is just to tag them so that you're not mailing especially if you do daily you don't want to be mailing dead weight for all that time so if you just tag them that they're inactive you don't mail them and then periodically you know once a month or every three months you try to activate them but yeah if depending on how you're calculating everything where you're just like you know what there I've tried a couple reactivation reactivation campaigns they're not responding they're no longer interested I'm probably in their spam folder unfortunately they're costing me a lot of money then yeah it's definitely going to help scrub those and if they're also the service provider as well if they don't have that we did a series of free contents and then that was the biggest launch in their company's history and the follow-up that was with a relatively unknown golf pro in phoenix martin chuck and I didn't have any pressure they're just like hey you know we just had this huge launch with this famous guy so you know that was awesome this is probably not going to do as well but you know hey we'll we'll hope for the best and we and uh we destroyed it this he's not ranked in the top golf digest list of top instructors or anything and we crushed the gary player launch by doubling the amount of content and massive list and they're mailing the same offer over and over again um based on special offers and stuff it would be tracking because they really don't know what's working the best you know they made they they know they made x amount that day but they don't know which email actually brought in the most amount of money and when I go when I go in and I put in that tracking that I can show them like look you you're you don't have the pulse of the market this email that you've tried several times is not doing jack squat and look how we changed it to fixing their slice now look at the results and stuff like that other times I would say the other big one would be remit remails and a lot of people like to do it on non-opens I don't I think the easiest the most effective way is just to do it on non-clips so what you do is you're changing the subject line you're changing up the email a little bit changing the hook and so you're basically hitting non-opens and non-clips all at the same time because if you go for non-opens then you're probably going to be missing some people that just didn't turn images on so I just go straight for non-clips okay all right so we've gotten a lot of great
